coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin nanofiltration membrane assembled by polyethyleneimine-grafted graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Chao
Li, Ya
Long, Mengying
Yang, Pengfei
Li, Yafei
Zheng, Yu
Zhang, Runnan
Su, Yanlei
Wu, Hong
Jiang, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: Embedding nanofillers into the selective layer to creating moderate nanopores is a promising strategy to obtain high-performance nanofiltration membranes. Herein, we synthesized polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the water phase monomers and prepared an ultrathin desalination membrane through interfacial polymerization (IP). The in situ embedded GQDs with superiorly uniform dispersion generate abundant nanopores in the membrane, and PEI chains fill the introduced nanopores, avoiding the formation of overlarge nanopores. Besides, the PEI-grafted GQDs (NGQDs) display a reduced diffusion rate during IP, rendering an ultrathin selective layer. Benefiting from the ultrathin thickness (∼6.5 nm), the abundant water pathways (specific surface area: 6.73 m2/g), and the increased pore size (∼0.82 nm), the PA-NGQD600 membrane exhibits competitive pure water permeance (38.5 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) and inorganic salt rejection (95.5% for Na2SO4). The permeance exceeds those of the most desalination membranes reported so far and is ∼3 times higher than that of the reported GQD-based desalination membrane. This work provides a facile strategy for creating abundant nanopores in membranes with ultrathin thickness by interfacial polymerization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Polyethyleneimine
Nanofiller
Nanofiltration
Desalination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the PA-NGQD membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication of the PA-NGQD membrane. (b) FTIR spectra of the h-PAN, PA-PE1600, PA-GQD/PEI600, and membranes PA-NGQD600. (c) XPS spectrum and C 1s high-resolution spectrum of the PA-NGQD600 membrane. (d) SEM images of the PA-NGQD600 membrane. (e) AFM topographical of the surface of the PA-NGQD600 membrane. (f) TEM image of the ultrathin section of the PA-NGQD600 membrane. (g) Diffusion coefficients of GQD and NGQD600.

TEM images of the (a) PA-PE1600, (b) PA-GQD/PEI600, and (c) PA-NGQD600 membranes. The inserts in the three images are the schematic diagrams of the PA-PE1600, PA-GQD/PEI600, and PA-NGQD600 membranes, respectively. (d) Specific surface areas, (e) MWCOs, and (f) pore size distribution of the PA-PE1600, PA-GQD/PEI600, and PA-NGQD600 membranes.

(a) Permeance and (b) rejection of the PA-PEI, PA-GQD/PEI600, and PA-NGQD membranes. (c) Performance comparison of the PA-NGQD600 membrane and the reported membranes. (d) Schematic diagram of mass transfer of the PA-NGQD600 membrane.

Stability evaluation of the PA-NGQD600 membrane. (a) Separation performances of the PA-NGQD600 membrane under various Na2SO4 concentrations. (b) Fluxes and rejection of the PA-NGQD600 membrane under various driving pressure. (c) Long-term stability of the PA-NGQD600 membrane for filtering 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin nanofiltration membrane assembled by polyethyleneimine-grafted graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Embedding nanofillers into the selective layer to creating moderate nanopores is a promising strategy to obtain high-performance nanofiltration membranes. Herein, we synthesized polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the water phase monomers and prepared an ultrathin desalination membrane through interfacial polymerization (IP). The in situ embedded GQDs with superiorly uniform dispersion generate abundant nanopores in the membrane, and PEI chains fill the introduced nanopores, avoiding the formation of overlarge nanopores. Besides, the PEI-grafted GQDs (NGQDs) display a reduced diffusion rate during IP, rendering an ultrathin selective layer. Benefiting from the ultrathin thickness (∼6.5 nm), the abundant water pathways (specific surface area: 6.73 m2/g), and the increased pore size (∼0.82 nm), the PA-NGQD600 membrane exhibits competitive pure water permeance (38.5 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) and inorganic salt rejection (95.5% for Na2SO4). The permeance exceeds those of the most desalination membranes reported so far and is ∼3 times higher than that of the reported GQD-based desalination membrane. This work provides a facile strategy for creating abundant nanopores in membranes with ultrathin thickness by interfacial polymerization.

ce:section.ce:para: The efficient treatment and resource utilization of saline wastewater are of crucial significance to alleviating the increasingly severe global water crisis and ecological environmental issues [1,2]. Nanofiltration membranes possessing a pore size of 0.5∼2.0 nm are showing a promising prospect in removing or separating multivalent salts [3–7]. The thin-film composite (TFC) polyamide (PA) membranes prepared via interfacial polymerization are currently studied nanofiltration membranes and have been commercialized [8–10]. In recent decades, researchers are making various efforts to break the trade-off between permeability and selectivity, which is a paramount and long-standing challenge for TFC PA membranes [11–14].

Embedding nanofiller into PA selective layers to fabricate thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, inspired by the structure of cell membranes, is one of the most direct and feasible strategies to break through the trade-off [15–17]. The nanofillers, for instance, covalent organic frameworks [18,19], metal-organic framework [20,21], graphene oxide [22,23], nanocapsules [15], cyclodextrin [24], etc., create water pathways through their inherent nanopores or the interfaces between nanofillers and PA network, thus improving the membrane permeability [25–27]. Thanks to the development of nanotechnology, the strategy is considered to be one of the most prospective technologies for manufacturing high-performance nanofiltration membranes [28,29]. Recently, researchers are trying to process the nanofillers into monomers to avoid the uneven filler distribution and the formation of non-selective defects induced by the low miscibility between the nanofillers and PA layers [30,31]. In addition, the strong chemical covalent interaction between nanofillers and the PA network eases the leaching of nanofiller during usage, which is also highlighted as a major challenge for the application of the TFN membranes [8,25].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a kind of zero-dimensional material with a feature size of ∼2 nm, possess a high specific surface area, excellent chemical stability, nontoxicity, and high mechanical strength [32,33]. Their graphene-like structure allows water molecules to transport with low friction [34,35]. Since 2018, GQDs and their derivatives have been used as the water phase monomers to fabricate TFN membranes [36,37]. In these membranes, it generally forms less cross-linked regions (also known as nanovoids) around GQDs [36]. Such nanovoid shows a huge capacity in enhancing performances for molecular sieving membranes [37,38]. Our previous work has reported a membrane prepared by GQDs with water permeance up to 244.7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 for removing dyes from water [37]. Very recently, GQD-based monomers are being explored to improve water permeance for desalination membranes [36]. However, it usually needs to increase the membrane thickness to obtain high water-salt selectivity, making the GQD-based desalination membranes uncompetitive in permeability (<10 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) [36]. To realize high permeability, it requires engineering the introduced nanovoid to an Angstrom scale [11]. This forces us to design the structure of the functionalized GQDs to control the size of nanopores.

Herein, polyethyleneimine (PEI) was selected to functionalize GQDs. The PEI-grafted GQDs (NGQDs) were used as the water phase monomers and performed interfacial polymerization with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on an ultrafiltration membrane. GQDs form plenty of less cross-linked regions in the membrane, achieving abundant nanopores. The PEI prevents the formation of overlarge nanovoids around GQDs, thereby engineering the pore size to a moderate one. We observed the distribution of GQDs in the membranes and revealed the influence of the molecular weight of PEI on the separation performances of the formed membranes. Meanwhile, we compared the homogeneity, pore structures, and separation performances of the membrane prepared via in situ embedding GQDs with that of the membrane fabricated by the blending of PEI and GQDs. Finally, stability and durability experiments were performed to demonstrate the application potential of the obtained membrane.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) membranes were supplied from Lanjing Membrane Engineering Co. Citric acid (CA), n-heptane, TMC, isobutanol, glucose, sucrose, raffinose, and β-cyclodextrin were provided by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. PEI, N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Institute. Deionized water was prepared through a water purification facility.

GQDs, as described in the supporting information (Fig. S1), were synthesized by the thermal cracking of CA as referred to in the previous work [39]. NGQDs were prepared by the EDC-NHS coupling reaction as illustrated in Fig. S2a (supporting information). In a typical process, 39.1 mg EDC and 49.8 mg NHS were added into 20 mL 20 wt% GQDs solution and magnetic stirring for 1 h. Subsequently, PEI was dissolved into the solution and was stirred for 24 h. The prepared solution was performed rotary evaporation to removed water and then poured into ethanol. The NGQDs are insoluble in ethanol (Fig. S2b, supporting information), and the product precipitated out in the mixed solution, which was followed by centrifugation to obtain the purified NGQDs. After three times of washing with ethanol and centrifugation, the final purified NGQDs were obtained after lyophilization. The subscript denotes the molecular weight of the PEI used.

The PA-NGQD nanofiltration membranes were fabricated by interfacial polymerization. First, the PAN ultrafiltration membrane was put into 30 mL 1 mol/L NaOH solution at 60°C and 20% relative humidity for 1 h. The hydrolyzed PAN (h-PAN) membrane, was washed with deionized water, and then, as demonstrated in Fig. S3 (supporting information), was placed into the 30 mL predetermined-concentration NGQD solution which pH was adjusted to 10.5. After soaking for 15 min, the membrane was taken out, and the liquid remaining on the membrane surface was gently absorbed with filter paper. Then, the membrane was immersed into 30 ml predetermined-concentration TMC solution for predetermined time. The resulting membrane was heated at 60°C for 10 min. The predetermined monomer concentration was 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, and 0.2 wt%, respectively. The predetermined reaction time was 1 min, 2 min, and 3 min, respectively. The PA-GQD/PEI600 and PA-PEI600 membranes were prepared with the aqueous monomers of the mixture of GQDs and PEI600 and PEI600, respectively, at 0.1 wt% monomer concentration and 2 min interfacial polymerization time.

Surface morphology was obtained by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Apreo S LoVac). Surface topography was examined by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Park XE-70). The thickness of the membranes and the distribution of the GQDs in the membranes were tested by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The chemical structures were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 560) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+). The grafting amount of PEI on NGQDs was carried out through a thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, NETZSCH TG209 F3). Zeta potential was evaluated by using an electrokinetic Analyzer (SurPASS). Specific surface area and total pore volume were obtained by a specific surface analyzer (MicrotracBEL, BELSORP-max). Diffusion coefficients, molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), pore size distribution, and separation performances were tested as described in the supporting information.

The separation performances of the membranes were characterized via a cross-flow filtration device with an effective membrane area of 1.54 cm2. The membranes were pre-compressed at 3 bar for 30 min to reach stable fluxes. The pure water permeance was tested at 2 bar and calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
P e r m e a n c e =
V
A × t × P
where V, A, t, and P are the volume of water filtrate (L), the effective membrane area (m2), the running time (h), and the transmembrane pressure, respectively.

The rejection of membranes was measured by filtering 4 different salt solutions, namely, Na2SO4, NaCl, MgSO4, and MgCl2. The concentration of the salt solutions was 1000 ppm. The salt rejection is calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
R e j e c t i o n =
(
1 −
c f
c 0
)
× 100 %
where c f and c 0 are the salt concentration of the filtrate and the feed. The salt concentration was obtained by measuring the conductivity of the solution with a conductivity meter (INESA DDS-307A).

NGQDs, as described in Fig. 1 a, were prepared through the EDC-NHS coupling reaction. PEI possessing abundant amine groups reacted with the carboxyl groups at the edge of GQDs under the activation of EDC and NHS [33,40]. The presence of the characteristic peak of amide bonds at 1570 cm-1 in the FTIR spectra of NGQDs (Fig. S4, supporting information), together with the appearance of the C–N peaks (285.7 eV, 400.8 eV) and NH2 peaks (398.7 eV) in the XPS spectra of NGQDs (Fig. S5, supporting information), indicate the successful modification of PEI on GQDs [41]. We used PEI with different molecular weights, namely, 600 Da, 1800 Da, 10000 Da, to modify GQDs, and all the obtained NGQDs present a dot-like structure (Fig. S6, supporting information). The particle size grows with the increase of PEI molecular weight. As shown in Fig. S6d (supporting information), the average particle size of NGQD increases from 2 nm to 6 nm, as the molecular weight of PEI rises from 600 Da to 10000 Da. TGA was employed to analyze the grafting amount of PEI. The mass proportion of PEI is 33.4 wt%, 34.0 wt%, and 38.3 wt% for NGQD600, NGQD1800, NGQD10000, respectively (Fig. S7, supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the PA-NGQD membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared NGQDs were used as the aqueous phase monomer and chemically assembled with TMC through interfacial polymerization on the h-PAN ultrafiltration membranes. The PA-NGQD600 membrane prepared at the monomer concentration of 0.1 wt% and interfacial polymerization time of 2 min is taken as an example to discuss the chemical structure and morphology. As depicted in Fig. 1a, the amino groups of NGQDs reacted with the acid chloride groups of TMC and formed a PA selective layer [36]. The stretching vibration peaks of the amide bond (-NH-CO) at 1623 cm-1 and 1570 cm-1 can be observed in the FTIR spectrum of the PA-NGQD600 membrane (Fig. 1b) [4]. The characteristic peaks of C–N (285.7 eV) and CO (287.6 eV) appear in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the PA-NGQD600 membrane (Fig. 1c) [30]. All the results declare the generation of PA on the h-PAN membrane. With the formation of the PA layer, the pores of the support membrane (Fig. S8, supporting information) are completely covered, and a dense surface with a roughness of 7.8 nm can be observed through SEM and AFM (Fig. 1d and e). By observing the cross-section of the PA-NGQD600 membrane under TEM (Fig. 1f), the thickness of the PA layer is roughly 6.5 nm. This value is far less than the previous studies (>50 nm) [31,36]. Modifying GQD with long-chain PEI reduces the diffusion rate of the monomer, which declines to 4.3 × 10-10 cm2/s of NGQD600 from 5.6 × 10-10 cm2/s of GQD (Fig. 1g, Fig. S9, supporting information), resulting in the ultrathin thickness according to the kinetic model [42,43]. This ultrathin selective layer endows the membrane with short mass transfer pathways, thereby facilitating the increase of permeability. For comparison, the GQD hybrid membrane (PA-GQD/PEI600) and pristine membrane (PA-PEI600) were fabricated. The characteristic peaks of amide bonds can be detected in the FTIR spectra of the two membranes (Fig. 1b), and both membranes present a dense and defect-free surface (Fig. S10, supporting information).

In terms of TFN membranes, the distribution of nanofillers in the membranes is of importance, which is remarkably affecting the creation of mass transfer pathways [26]. Membranes prepared by the free-standing interfacial polymerization technology were observed under TEM to investigate the distribution of the GQDs across the prepared membranes. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the PA-NGQD600 membrane is composed of uniformly distributed GQDs, which is confirmed by the feature lattice fringes (interplanar spacing: ∼0.24 nm). While in the TEM photo of the PA-GQD/PEI600 membrane (Fig. 2b), dark spots can be seen, demonstrating that GQDs are unevenly embedded and partial agglomerate in the membrane. The introduction of GQDs creates nanopores in the PA networks [35]， which is verified by the higher specific surface area and total pore volume of the PA-NGQD600 and PA-GQD/PEI600 membranes than that of the PA-PEI600 membrane (Figure 2d and Fig. S11, supporting information). More importantly, the PA-NGQD600 membrane shows increased specific surface area (5.26 m2/g) than that of the PA-GQD/PEI600 membrane (4.57 m2/g), which illustrates that optimization in GQD dispersion facilitates the construction of more nanopores. In order to further verify this speculation, we used solutes with different molecular weights, as listed in Table S1 (supporting information), as probe molecules to test the MWCOs of the membranes (Fig. 2e), and the pore size distribution was calculated as shown in Fig. 2f. With the introduction of GQDs, both the MWCO and the pore size of the membranes increase, confirming that GQDs create nanopores in the membrane. The PA-NGQD600 membrane exhibits larger MWCO (537 Da) and pore size (Rmean = 0.41 nm) compared with those of the PA-GQD/PEI600 membrane (MWCO = 306 Da, Rmean = 0.32 nm). These support the inference that the PA-NGQD600 membrane with more evenly distributed creates more nanopores in the membranes.

The separation performances are evaluated through cross-flow filtration. We investigated the effects of monomer concentration and interfacial polymerization time on membrane separation performances. As the monomer concentration and reaction time increase, the membrane tends to become dense and the permeance decreases. When the monomer concentration is 0.1 wt% and the reaction time is 2 min, the as-prepared membrane shows the optimal separation performance (Fig. S12, supporting information). Therefore, we chose the 0.1 wt% monomer concentration and 2 min interfacial polymerization time as the reaction condition to prepare membranes for the following evaluation. As presented in Fig. 3 a, due to the introduction of GQDs, the PA-GQD/PEI600 and PA-NGQD600 membranes show higher pure water permeance, namely, 14.3 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and 38.5 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, than that of the PA-PEI600 membrane (4.6 L m-2 h-1 bar-1). Benefiting from the more nanopores introduced by more uniformly dispersed GQDs, the permeance of the PA-NGQD600 membrane is almost 2.7 times higher than that of the GQD/PEI600 membrane. We tried to increase the molecular weight of PEI to explore the effect of PEI molecular weight on membrane separation performances. The permeance declines with the PEI molecular weight, which may be related to the different membrane structures. On one hand, increasing the molecular weight of PEI increases the mass ratio of PEI in the monomer NGQDs (discussed in Section 3.1), which increases the PEI part of the prepared membrane, making the membrane denser. On the other hand, increasing the molecular weight of PEI decreases the interface of NGQDs. An earlier study published by Jiang et al. pointed out that GQD grafted by PEI with different molecular weights formed various structures [41]. When GQDs are modified with PEI600, the PEI600 will connect multiple GQDs and form a multi-core network structure. While PEI with molecular weight over 1800 Da will wrap GQDs and generate a single-core structure [41]. It can be inferred that the wrapped GQDs lose their interface area and generate small nanovoids, which is not conducive to the creation of water pathways, resulting in the inferior permeance.

Four kinds of inorganic salts are used for estimating the ionic rejection of the membranes. After introducing nanopores by adding GQDs, the membrane shows a negligible change in the rejection of divalent salts compare with the PA-PEI600 membrane (Fig. 3b). The rejection of the PA-GQD/PEI600 and PA-NGQD600 membrane are MgCl2 (95.7%) > MgSO4 (58.7%) > Na2SO4 (49.4%) > NaCl (32.1%) and Na2SO4 (95.5%) > MgSO4 (65.4%) > MgCl2 (22.5%) > NaCl (22.3%), respectively. The membranes exhibit differences in ions with different charges. This is mainly caused by the Donnan effect produced by the charged surfaces (Fig. S13, supporting information). The positively charged PA-GQD/PEI600 membrane possesses higher rejection of MgCl2, and the negatively charged PA-NGQD600 membrane shows higher rejection of Na2SO4. With the increase of PEI molecular weight, the PA-NGQD1800 membrane rejects more ions than the PA-NGQD600 membrane because of the denser network generated by the wrapped GQDs. The PA-NGQD10000 membrane exhibits inferior ion rejection arising from the loose and thick active layer. To be specific, NGQD10000 is flocculent in the aqueous solution. Its uneven distribution and migration on the support membrane result in that the self-sealing and self-healing behaviors cannot be completely achieved during the interfacial polymerization, thus forming the loose and thick PA layer.

Considering permeance and selectivity, the PA-NGQD600 membrane exhibits prominent separation performances. Compared with the performances of various nanofiltration membranes reported so far [6,7,10,14,19,23,35,44–50], as displayed in Fig. 3c, the PA-NGQD600 membrane possesses competitive permeance and salt rejection. Compared with the membranes assembled with small-molecule-grafted GQDs, the separation performances are also greatly improved almost 3 times under similar salt rejection [36]. The superior separation performances can be related to the numerous and short mass transfer paths as well as the selective pore structure as illustrated in Fig. 3d. Specifically, the slow diffusion rate of the aqueous monomer (NGQD600) slumps the thickness of the as-prepared membrane, according to the kinetic model delivered by Freger [42,43]. This greatly shortened mass transfer pathways. Besides, PEI-functionalized GQDs directly perform interfacial polymerization with TMC, making it more uniformly distributed in the PA network, without agglomeration. Numerous discrete GQDs in the membrane create many organic-inorganic interfaces, thus providing abundant mass transfer pathways. More importantly, the monomer structure with flexible long-chain PEI avoids the formation of non-selective low cross-linking regions, so that the prepared membrane is defect-free and exhibits high selectivity under such low thickness.

The stability of the membrane is one of the indispensable and fundamental capability for practical applications [51]. The stability of the PA-NGQD600 membrane was investigated by testing the flux and rejection of inorganic salts under different inorganic salt concentrations and operating pressures. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the flux of the membrane gradually decreases with the concentration of the salt solution. This is caused by concentration polarization [52]. The membrane maintains outstanding rejection in high-concentration salt solutions. It can still reject over 91.7% Na2SO4 under the concentration of 6000 ppm, indicating usability in a wide salt concentration range. Fig. 4b shows the flux and rejection of the PA-NGQD600 membrane varied with various driving pressures. The flux ascends linearly with the driving pressure, and the rejection shows a negligible change under different pressures. These signify that the PA network structure of the PA-NGQD600 membrane possesses extremely high stability under pressure. The decrease in separation performances stemmed from the leaching of nanofillers during filtration is a grant concern [25]. We tested the performances of the PA-NGQD600 membrane during long-term filtration of 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution. In the three-week continuous filtration, the permeance and the rejection of the membrane are almost stable, which suggests the firm network structure. GQDs are embedded in the membrane by covalent bonds and have a strong interaction with the polyamide network, avoiding the leaching of GQDs and the collapse of the internal structure during usage. This stable structure ensures that the membrane can work continuously for a long time.

In summary, we explored PEI grafted GQDs as the aqueous phase monomer to react with TMC through interfacial polymerization on an h-PAN ultrafiltration membrane to fabricate a desalination membrane. After modifying with PEI600, the diffusion rate of NGQD600 reduced, thus obtaining an ultrathin selective layer with a thickness of ∼6.5 nm. More importantly, the PA-NGQD600 with in situ embedded GQDs show a more uniform GQD dispersion than that of the PA-NGQD membrane with physical embedded GQDs, resulting in more nanopores. The MWCO and mean pore diameter of the PA-NGQD600 membrane are 537 Da and 0.82 nm, respectively. By adjusting the molecular weight of PEI, the PA-NGQD600 membrane prepared by PEI600 modified GQDs exhibits the most competitive separation performances, which displays Na2SO4 rejection of 95.5% and pure water permeance of 38.5 L m-2 h-1 bar-1. The permeance exceeds those of most desalination membranes reported so far and is 3 times higher than that of the reported GQD-based desalination membrane. Besides, the PA-NGQD600 membrane can be applied in a wide range of salt concentration and driving pressure and possesses excellent long-term stability. This facile strategy provides a method and insights for constructing abundant and abundant nanopores with moderate pore size in the nanofiltration membranes with ultrathin thickness.

Chao Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Ya Li: Methodology, Investigation. Mengying Long: Characterization. Pengfei Yang: Diagram, Software. Yafei Li: Characterization. Yu Zheng: Characterization. Runnan Zhang: Data curation. Yanlei Su: Supervision. Hong Wu: Data curation. Zhongyi Jiang: Project design, final polishing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21961142013, 21878217, 21878215, 91934302, 22008172), and Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2021C03173) for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2021.119944.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Science and technology for water purification in the coming decades
Nature
